introduction: The Battle of Stalingrad was one of the most important and largest battles on the Eastern Front of World War II. In the battle for the city of Stalingrad (in 1961 the city was renamed back to Volgograd) in the period from August 23, 1942 to February 2, 1943, the 4th and 6th armies of the German Wehrmacht clashed, reinforced by Romanian, Italian, Hungarian and Croatian troops. , with the Red Army. The battle for the city bearing Stalin's name was waged not only for military-strategic, but also for prestigious reasons. After long battles in which victory alternated to one side or the other, the Red Army surrounded a cauldron around Stalingrad around 250,000 enemy soldiers, of whom 100,000 fell, 47,000 were wounded and about 91,000 were captured. The crushing defeat of the German side and the dispersal of the 6th Army under the command of General Friedrich Paulus were a turning point in development on the Eastern Front. In Nazi Germany, the event was propagandistically interpreted as a national tragedy, and the declining belief in victory was to lift the declaration of a "total war." Hitler even refused to accept Paulus' surrender and, on the contrary, promoted him to the rank of field marshal and with him 117 other officers to the next higher ranks. In the Soviet Union, the battle became an example of the heroism and determination of Soviet citizens in the fight against the enemy. As such, it has inspired many artists to create works of art and journalistic testimony about this battle. The Soviet-made documentary "Battle of Stalingrad" contains in particular a detailed analysis of military operations using illustrative map diagrams.
